distinct-values-for-report-categories.label: Distinct Values for Report Categories
distinct-values-for-report-categories.summary: Learn how to use the DISTINCT keyword in SQL to identify unique categories in a dataset, which is essential for creating clear and concise reports.

try_distinct_values_for_report_categorie.title: Try It: Unique Categories
try_distinct_values_for_report_categorie.prompt: Write a SQL query to list all unique categories from the `sales_reporting` table. This will help you understand the variety of products available.
try_distinct_values_for_report_categorie.hint: Use the lesson explanation and the wording of this question to narrow the answer.

help.concept: This support text was repaired because the original wording revealed the answer too directly.
help.hint_1: Compare the question to the lesson example and remove details that do not match the requested concept.
help.hint_2: Use the role or evidence named in the prompt instead of relying on answer wording.

try_distinct_values_for_report_categorie.title: Try It: Unique Region-Category Pairs
try_distinct_values_for_report_categorie.prompt: Write a SQL query to find all unique combinations of region and category from the `sales_reporting` table. This will help you see how products are distributed across regions.
try_distinct_values_for_report_categorie.hint: Use the lesson explanation and the wording of this question to narrow the answer.

help.concept: This support text was repaired because the original wording revealed the answer too directly.
help.hint_1: Compare the question to the lesson example and remove details that do not match the requested concept.
help.hint_2: Use the role or evidence named in the prompt instead of relying on answer wording.

try_distinct_values_for_report_categorie.solutionCode:
SELECT DISTINCT region, category
FROM sales_reporting;

single-choice-1.title: Purpose of DISTINCT
single-choice-1.prompt: What is the primary purpose of the DISTINCT keyword in SQL?
single-choice-1.hint: Think about what DISTINCT does to duplicate rows.

help.concept: DISTINCT is used to eliminate duplicate rows from the result set.
help.hint_1: DISTINCT ensures that each row in the result set is unique.
help.hint_2: DISTINCT is applied to the columns specified in the SELECT statement.

options.a: To sort the data in ascending order
options.b: To remove duplicate rows from the result set
options.c: To calculate the sum of a column
options.d: To filter rows based on a condition

multi-choice-1.title: Using DISTINCT with Multiple Columns
multi-choice-1.prompt: Which of the following statements about using DISTINCT with multiple columns are true? Select all that apply.
multi-choice-1.hint: Consider how DISTINCT affects the result set when multiple columns are involved.

help.concept: DISTINCT can be used with multiple columns to find unique combinations of values.
help.hint_1: DISTINCT applies to the combination of all selected columns.
help.hint_2: Each unique combination of the selected columns will appear once in the result set.

options.a: DISTINCT can only be used with one column at a time
options.b: DISTINCT will return unique combinations of the selected columns
options.c: Using DISTINCT with multiple columns will return all possible combinations
options.d: DISTINCT can help identify unique pairs or sets of data

fill-blank-choice-1.title: DISTINCT Keyword Usage
fill-blank-choice-1.prompt: Complete the SQL statement to find unique product names from the sales_reporting table: `SELECT [blank1] product_name FROM sales_reporting;`

help.concept: the missing term is used to ensure each row in the result set is unique.
help.hint_1: the missing term is placed right after SELECT to affect the result set.
help.hint_2: the missing term ensures that only unique product names are returned.

single-choice-2.title: DISTINCT and Aliases
single-choice-2.prompt: How does using an alias with DISTINCT affect the result set?
single-choice-2.hint: Consider what an alias does to the column name in the result set.

help.concept: An alias renames a column in the result set but does not affect the DISTINCT operation.
help.hint_1: DISTINCT removes duplicates, while an alias changes the column header.
help.hint_2: The alias is applied after DISTINCT has filtered the rows.

options.a: It changes the column name in the result set
options.b: It affects which rows are considered duplicates
options.c: It changes the data type of the column
options.d: It has no effect on the DISTINCT operation

multi-choice-2.title: DISTINCT Limitations
multi-choice-2.prompt: Which of the following are limitations of using DISTINCT in SQL? Select all that apply.
multi-choice-2.hint: Think about what DISTINCT can and cannot do.

help.concept: DISTINCT is limited to removing duplicate rows based on the selected columns.
help.hint_1: DISTINCT does not perform calculations or transformations.
help.hint_2: DISTINCT cannot be used to filter rows based on conditions.

options.b: DISTINCT cannot filter rows based on conditions
options.c: DISTINCT can only be used with numeric columns
options.d: DISTINCT cannot be used with ORDER BY

sketch0.title: Understanding DISTINCT in SQL
sketch0.bodyMarkdown:
The `DISTINCT` keyword in SQL is used to remove duplicate rows from the result set. This is particularly useful when you want to list unique values from a column. For example, if you have a table of sales data and you want to find out all the unique product categories, you can use `DISTINCT` to achieve this.

```sql
SELECT DISTINCT category
FROM sales_reporting;
```

This query will return a list of all unique categories in the `sales_reporting` table. It's important to note that `DISTINCT` applies to all columns in the SELECT statement, so if you select multiple columns, the combination of all columns must be unique for a row to be included in the result.

sketch1.title: Using DISTINCT with Multiple Columns
sketch1.bodyMarkdown:
When using `DISTINCT` with multiple columns, SQL will return unique combinations of the specified columns. This can be useful when you want to see unique pairs or sets of data.

For example, if you want to find unique combinations of regions and categories in the `sales_reporting` table, you can write:

```sql
SELECT DISTINCT region, category
FROM sales_reporting;
```

This query will return each unique pair of region and category. It's a powerful way to understand the diversity of data combinations in your dataset.
